EXPERIENCES IN LONG-DISTANCE RELATIONSHIPS

  • Show All Code
  • Hide All Code
  • View Source

PSY 120L Reproducible Analysis Report

Author

ALLISON SALES

Published

June 6, 2026

About This Report

The following demonstrates the workflow for the study, “Experiences in Long-Distance Relationships.” This report documents the data preparation, variable construction, descriptive statistics, primary analyses, and figures for this study.

The report is intended to accompany a manuscript-style research paper. It is not the full paper itself. Instead, it shows how the reported analyses were produced from the dataset using a transparent and reproducible Quarto workflow.

Study Overview

This study examines how perceived communication responsiveness in long-distance relationships affect relationship satisfaction, and if this association differs based on attachment style and prior experience in long-distance relationships.

The design included one independent variable and two moderators:

  • Communication responsiveness condition: high communication responsiveness versus low communication responsiveness
  • Attachment style: low attachment insecurity versus high attachment insecurity
  • Prior LDR experience: experience versus no experience

The dependent variable was relationship satisfaction

The first primary analyses tested whether perceived communication responsiveness, attachment insecurity, and their interaction predicted relationship satisfaction. The attachment insecurity moderator included four subscales: overall insecurity, dependence insecurity, attachment anxiety, and closeness discomfort.

The second analyses evaluated whether perceived communication responsiveness, prior LDR experience, and their interaction predicted relationship satisfaction.

Setup and Software Environment

The purpose of this section is to load packages used for data preparation, scale construction, statistical modeling, estimated marginal means, and figures.

Load Packages

Show code 
library(tidyverse)  # data manipulation + plotting
library(psych)      # scale construction + reliability
library(afex)       # ANOVA models + effect sizes
library(emmeans)    # estimated marginal means + confidence intervals

Data Source and Analysis Dataset

The dataset was imported from the project data folder. The imported dataset was saved as study_raw so that the original file remained unchanged throughout the report.

A smaller analysis dataset, saved as study_data, was then created for the current analyses. This dataset retained the study condition variables, demographic variables, Relationship Assessment Scale items, Adult Attachment Scale items, and prior LDR experience items needed for variable preparation and analysis.

Import Dataset

Show code 
study_raw <- read_csv("data/long_distance_data.csv")

Check Imported Dataset

Show code 
dim(study_raw)
[1] 167  39
Show code 
names(study_raw)
 [1] "StartDate"             "EndDate"               "Duration (in seconds)"
 [4] "Finished"              "RecordedDate"          "Consent Form"

[10] "RAS3"                  "RAS4"                  "RAS5"

Checking the imported dataset confirms that the data loaded successfully, shows the number of rows and columns, and displays the correct variable names available in the original dataset.

Create Analysis Dataset

Show code 
study_vars <- c(
  "condition", "age", "gender", 
  
  
  "MC1",
  
  "RAS1", "RAS2", "RAS3", "RAS4", "RAS5", "RAS6", "RAS7",
  
  "AAS1", "AAS2", "AAS3", "AAS4", "AAS5", "AAS6",
  "AAS7", "AAS8", "AAS9", "AAS10", "AAS11", "AAS12",
  "AAS13", "AAS14", "AAS15", "AAS16", "AAS17", "AAS18",
  
  "relationship_exp",
  "relationship_length"
)

study_data <- study_raw %>%
  select(all_of(study_vars)) %>%
  mutate(
    across(everything(), as.numeric)
  )

Check Analysis Dataset

Show code 
length(study_vars)
[1] 31
Show code 
dim(study_data)
[1] 167  31
Show code 
names(study_data)
 [1] "condition"           "age"                 "gender"             
 [4] "MC1"                 "RAS1"                "RAS2"               
 [7] "RAS3"                "RAS4"                "RAS5"               
[10] "RAS6"                "RAS7"                "AAS1"               
[13] "AAS2"                "AAS3"                "AAS4"               
[16] "AAS5"                "AAS6"                "AAS7"               
[19] "AAS8"                "AAS9"                "AAS10"              
[22] "AAS11"               "AAS12"               "AAS13"              
[25] "AAS14"               "AAS15"               "AAS16"              
[28] "AAS17"               "AAS18"               "relationship_exp"   
[31] "relationship_length"

The analysis dataset contains the variables required for the report. The checks confirm the number of selected variables, the dimensions of the analysis dataset, and the final variable names carried forward into the preparation and analysis sections.

Variable Preparation

This section prepares the variables used in the primary analyses. The original imported dataset was left unchanged; all prepared variables were created in the working analysis dataset, study_data.

Prepare Participant ID and Experimental Condition

Show code 
study_data <- study_data %>%
  mutate(
    id = row_number(),

    condition = factor(
      condition,
      levels = c(0, 1),
      labels = c("Low", "High")
    ),

    relationship_exp = factor(
      relationship_exp,
      levels = c(0, 1),
      labels = c("No", "Yes")
    ),

    gender = factor(
      gender,
      levels = c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5),
      labels = c(
        "Female",
        "Male",
        "Transgender",
        "Prefer not to say",
        "Other"
      )
    )
  )

A participant ID variable was created so that each row in the analysis dataset had a unique identifier. The experiemental condition variable was recoded as a labeled factor with two levels: Low and High. The moderator, prior LDR experience was also recoded as a labels factor with two levles: No and Yes.

Check Experimental Condition Coding

Show code 
study_data %>%
  count(condition) %>%
  knitr::kable()
condition n
Low 83
High 84
Show code 
study_data %>%
  count(relationship_exp) %>%
  knitr::kable()
relationship_exp n
No 71
Yes 95
NA 1
Show code 
study_data %>%
  count(gender) %>%
  knitr::kable()
gender n
Female 132
Male 28
Transgender 1
Prefer not to say 1
Other 4
NA 1

The condition check confirms the number of participants assigned to each communication responsivenss condition. The moderator check also confirms the number of participants that have and do not have LDR experience. Lastly, gender is confirmed to analyze demographics.

Check Prepared Analysis Dataset

Show code 
dim(study_data)
[1] 167  32
Show code 
names(study_data)
 [1] "condition"           "age"                 "gender"             
 [4] "MC1"                 "RAS1"                "RAS2"               
 [7] "RAS3"                "RAS4"                "RAS5"               
[10] "RAS6"                "RAS7"                "AAS1"               
[13] "AAS2"                "AAS3"                "AAS4"               
[16] "AAS5"                "AAS6"                "AAS7"               
[19] "AAS8"                "AAS9"                "AAS10"              
[22] "AAS11"               "AAS12"               "AAS13"              
[25] "AAS14"               "AAS15"               "AAS16"              
[28] "AAS17"               "AAS18"               "relationship_exp"   
[31] "relationship_length" "id"

The prepared analysis dataset contains the variables needed for scale construction, descriptive statistics, and primary analyses.

Reverse-Code Scale Items

Items requiring reverse coding were transformed before scale scores were computed. Because the items in both scales used a 1–5 response scale, reverse-coded items were created by subtracting each original item response from 6.

Show code 
study_data <- study_data %>%
  mutate(
    # Reverse-code RAS items
    # Original scale: 1 to 5
    # Higher recoded scores = higher relationship satisfaction
    RAS4r = 6 - RAS4,
    RAS7r = 6 - RAS7,

    # Reverse-code AAS items
    # Original scale: 1 to 5
    # Higher recoded scores = higher attachment dimension score
    AAS3r  = 6 - AAS3,
    AAS4r  = 6 - AAS4,
    AAS7r  = 6 - AAS7,
    AAS13r = 6 - AAS13,
    AAS14r = 6 - AAS14,
    AAS17r = 6 - AAS17
  )

Check Reverse Coding

Show code 
study_data %>%
  select(RAS4, RAS4r, RAS7, RAS7r, AAS3, AAS3r, AAS7, AAS7r) %>%
  head(10) %>%
  knitr::kable()
RAS4 RAS4r RAS7 RAS7r AAS3 AAS3r AAS7 AAS7r
4 2 4 2 2 4 4 2
1 5 1 5 1 5 5 1
1 5 2 4 4 2 3 3
3 3 4 2 4 2 4 2
3 3 3 3 3 3 2 4
4 2 4 2 5 1 1 5
2 4 3 3 3 3 2 4
1 5 1 5 3 3 2 4
3 3 3 3 3 3 3 3
5 1 1 5 5 1 4 2

Create Scale Scores

Scale item lists were defined and used to compute Relationship Assessment and Adult Attachment scale scores.

Show code 
ras_items <- c("RAS1", "RAS2", "RAS3", "RAS4r", "RAS5", "RAS6", "RAS7r")

aas_total_items <- c("AAS1", "AAS2", "AAS3r", "AAS4r", "AAS5", 
                     "AAS6", "AAS7r", "AAS8", "AAS9", "AAS10", 
                     "AAS11", "AAS12", "AAS13r", "AAS14r", "AAS15", 
                     "AAS16", "AAS17r", "AAS18")

aas_depend_items <- c("AAS1", "AAS2", "AAS3r", "AAS4r", "AAS5", "AAS6")

aas_anxiety_items <- c("AAS7r", "AAS8", "AAS9", "AAS10", "AAS11", "AAS12")

aas_close_items <- c("AAS13r", "AAS14r", "AAS15", "AAS16", "AAS17r", "AAS18")

study_data <- study_data %>%
  mutate(
    ras_scale = rowMeans(select(., all_of(ras_items)), na.rm = TRUE),
    aas_total_scale = rowMeans(select(., all_of(aas_total_items)), na.rm = TRUE),
    aas_depend_scale = rowMeans(select(., all_of(aas_depend_items)), na.rm = TRUE),
    aas_anxiety_scale = rowMeans(select(., all_of(aas_anxiety_items)), na.rm = TRUE),
    aas_close_scale = rowMeans(select(., all_of(aas_close_items)), na.rm = TRUE)
  )

Check Scale Scores

Show code 
study_data %>%
  select(
    ras_scale,
    aas_total_scale,
    aas_depend_scale,
    aas_anxiety_scale,
    aas_close_scale
  ) %>%
  summary()
   ras_scale     aas_total_scale aas_depend_scale aas_anxiety_scale
 Min.   :1.000   Min.   :1.222   Min.   :1.167    Min.   :1.167    
 1st Qu.:2.571   1st Qu.:2.500   1st Qu.:2.500    1st Qu.:2.167    
 Median :3.286   Median :2.833   Median :3.000    Median :2.667    
 Mean   :3.293   Mean   :2.793   Mean   :2.990    Mean   :2.690    
 3rd Qu.:4.071   3rd Qu.:3.111   3rd Qu.:3.333    3rd Qu.:3.167    
 Max.   :5.000   Max.   :4.056   Max.   :4.833    Max.   :4.333    
 aas_close_scale
 Min.   :1.000  
 1st Qu.:2.333  
 Median :2.667  
 Mean   :2.701  
 3rd Qu.:3.167  
 Max.   :4.500
Show code 
study_data %>%
  summarise(
    ras_scale_missing = mean(is.na(ras_scale)),
    aas_total_scale_missing = mean(is.na(aas_total_scale)),
    aas_depend_scale_missing = mean(is.na(aas_depend_scale)),
    aas_anxiety_scale_missing = mean(is.na(aas_anxiety_scale)),
    aas_close_scale_missing = mean(is.na(aas_close_scale))
  ) %>%
  knitr::kable()
ras_scale_missing aas_total_scale_missing aas_depend_scale_missing aas_anxiety_scale_missing aas_close_scale_missing
0 0 0 0 0

The scale-score checks confirm that the computed scale scores fall within the expected response range and show whether any scale scores are missing.

Create Attachment Groups

A Lower/Higher attachment insecurity grouping variable was created for the planned 2 × 2 ANOVA models. Participants with attachment scores at or below the sample median were assigned to the Lower Attachment Insecurity group, and participants above the sample median were assigned to the Higher Attachment Insecurity group. The same was also done for Lower/High dependence insecurity, attachment anxiety, and closeness discomfort.

Show code 
# Create median values for the attachment scale scores
aas_total_median <- median(study_data$aas_total_scale, na.rm = TRUE)
aas_depend_median <- median(study_data$aas_depend_scale, na.rm = TRUE)
aas_anxiety_median <- median(study_data$aas_anxiety_scale, na.rm = TRUE)
aas_close_median <- median(study_data$aas_close_scale, na.rm = TRUE)

# Create median-split attachment groups
study_data <- study_data %>%
  mutate(
    aas_total_group = if_else(
      aas_total_scale <= aas_total_median,
      "Lower attachment insecurity",
      "Higher attachment insecurity"
    ),
    aas_total_group = factor(
      aas_total_group,
      levels = c("Lower attachment insecurity", "Higher attachment insecurity")
    ),

    aas_depend_group = if_else(
      aas_depend_scale <= aas_depend_median,
      "Lower dependence insecurity",
      "Higher dependence insecurity"
    ),
    aas_depend_group = factor(
      aas_depend_group,
      levels = c("Lower dependence insecurity", "Higher dependence insecurity")
    ),

    aas_anxiety_group = if_else(
      aas_anxiety_scale <= aas_anxiety_median,
      "Lower attachment anxiety",
      "Higher attachment anxiety"
    ),
    aas_anxiety_group = factor(
      aas_anxiety_group,
      levels = c("Lower attachment anxiety", "Higher attachment anxiety")
    ),

    aas_close_group = if_else(
      aas_close_scale <= aas_close_median,
      "Lower closeness discomfort",
      "Higher closeness discomfort"
    ),
    aas_close_group = factor(
      aas_close_group,
      levels = c("Lower closeness discomfort", "Higher closeness discomfort")
    )
  )

# Check final attachment group sizes
study_data %>%
  count(aas_total_group) %>%
  knitr::kable()
aas_total_group n
Lower attachment insecurity 88
Higher attachment insecurity 79
Show code 
study_data %>%
  count(aas_depend_group) %>%
  knitr::kable()
aas_depend_group n
Lower dependence insecurity 98
Higher dependence insecurity 69
Show code 
study_data %>%
  count(aas_anxiety_group) %>%
  knitr::kable()
aas_anxiety_group n
Lower attachment anxiety 87
Higher attachment anxiety 80
Show code 
study_data %>%
  count(aas_close_group) %>%
  knitr::kable()
aas_close_group n
Lower closeness discomfort 93
Higher closeness discomfort 74

The median split created the attachment grouping variable used in the primary ANOVA models. The output reports the median value used for the split and the number of participants assigned to each attachment group.

Check Scale Reliability

Internal consistency was estimated for the Relationship Assessment and Adult Attachment scales before the scale scores were used in the primary analyses

Show code 
# Create item-only data frames for each multi-item rating scale
ras_item_df <- study_data %>% 
  select(all_of(ras_items))

aas_total_item_df <- study_data %>% 
  select(all_of(aas_total_items))

aas_depend_item_df <- study_data %>% 
  select(all_of(aas_depend_items))

aas_anxiety_item_df <- study_data %>% 
  select(all_of(aas_anxiety_items))

aas_close_item_df <- study_data %>% 
  select(all_of(aas_close_items))

# Compute internal consistency estimates
ras_alpha <- psych::alpha(ras_item_df)
ras_omega <- psych::omega(ras_item_df, plot = FALSE)

aas_total_alpha <- psych::alpha(aas_total_item_df)
Some items ( AAS11 ) were negatively correlated with the first principal component and 
probably should be reversed.  
To do this, run the function again with the 'check.keys=TRUE' option
Show code 
aas_total_omega <- psych::omega(aas_total_item_df, plot = FALSE)

aas_depend_alpha <- psych::alpha(aas_depend_item_df)
aas_depend_omega <- psych::omega(aas_depend_item_df, plot = FALSE)

aas_anxiety_alpha <- psych::alpha(aas_anxiety_item_df)
aas_anxiety_omega <- psych::omega(aas_anxiety_item_df, plot = FALSE)

aas_close_alpha <- psych::alpha(aas_close_item_df)
aas_close_omega <- psych::omega(aas_close_item_df, plot = FALSE)

# Print only alpha and omega coefficients
tibble(
  scale = c(
    "Relationship Assessment Scale",
    "AAS Overall Insecurity",
    "AAS Dependence Insecurity",
    "AAS Attachment Anxiety",
    "AAS Closeness Discomfort"
  ),
  alpha = c(
    ras_alpha$total$raw_alpha,
    aas_total_alpha$total$raw_alpha,
    aas_depend_alpha$total$raw_alpha,
    aas_anxiety_alpha$total$raw_alpha,
    aas_close_alpha$total$raw_alpha
  ),
  omega = c(
    ras_omega$omega.tot,
    aas_total_omega$omega.tot,
    aas_depend_omega$omega.tot,
    aas_anxiety_omega$omega.tot,
    aas_close_omega$omega.tot
  )
) %>%
  knitr::kable(digits = 2)
scale alpha omega
Relationship Assessment Scale 0.92 0.96
AAS Overall Insecurity 0.78 0.83
AAS Dependence Insecurity 0.77 0.86
AAS Attachment Anxiety 0.68 0.80
AAS Closeness Discomfort 0.69 0.83

The reliability table reports Cronbach’s alpha and McDonald’s omega for each scale used in the analysis.

Descriptive Statistics

This section summarizes the scale scores and sample composition before the primary hypothesis tests.

Scale Score Descriptives

Show code 
study_data %>%
  select(
    ras_scale,
    aas_total_scale,
    aas_depend_scale,
    aas_anxiety_scale,
    aas_close_scale
  ) %>%
  psych::describe() %>%
  knitr::kable(digits = 2)
vars n mean sd median trimmed mad min max range skew kurtosis se
ras_scale 1 167 3.29 1.02 3.29 3.30 1.06 1.00 5.00 4.00 -0.08 -0.96 0.08
aas_total_scale 2 167 2.79 0.48 2.83 2.81 0.41 1.22 4.06 2.83 -0.32 0.51 0.04
aas_depend_scale 3 167 2.99 0.69 3.00 2.98 0.74 1.17 4.83 3.67 0.06 0.08 0.05
aas_anxiety_scale 4 167 2.69 0.68 2.67 2.69 0.74 1.17 4.33 3.17 0.04 -0.58 0.05
aas_close_scale 5 167 2.70 0.62 2.67 2.70 0.49 1.00 4.50 3.50 0.03 0.38 0.05

The table reports descriptive statistics for the Relationship Assessment and Adult Attachment scale scores used in the primary analyses.

Sample Composition by Age and Gender

Show code 
# Recode age categories so the Qualtrics numeric codes show as actual age labels
study_data <- study_data %>%
  mutate(
    age = factor(
      age,
      levels = c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8),
      labels = c("18", "19", "20", "21", "22", "23", "24", "Other")
    )
  )

# Summarize age categories
study_data %>%
  count(age) %>%
  mutate(percent = round(100 * n / sum(n), 1)) %>%
  knitr::kable(
    col.names = c("Age", "n", "Percent"),
    caption = "Sample Composition by Age"
  )

    caption = "Sample Composition by Gender"

# Summarize experimental condition
study_data %>%
  count(condition) %>%
  mutate(percent = round(100 * n / sum(n), 1)) %>%

    col.names = c("Condition", "n", "Percent"),
    caption = "Sample Composition by Experimental Condition"
  )
Sample Composition by Experimental Condition
Condition n Percent
Low 83 49.7
High 84 50.3

The tables report the number and percentage of participants in each age, gender, prior LDR experience, and experimental condition category.

Correlations Among the DVs

This section tests the correlation among the continuous variables in the study, which are the manipulation check question, Relationship Assessment Scale, AAS Overall Insecurity, AAS Dependence Insecurity, AAS Attachment Anxiety, and AAS Closeness Discomfort.

Show code 
# Correlations among the main continuous study variables
psych::corr.test(
  study_data %>% 
    select(
      MC1,
      ras_scale,
      aas_total_scale,
      aas_depend_scale,
      aas_anxiety_scale,
      aas_close_scale
    )
)
Call:psych::corr.test(x = study_data %>% select(MC1, ras_scale, aas_total_scale, 
    aas_depend_scale, aas_anxiety_scale, aas_close_scale))
Correlation matrix 
                    MC1 ras_scale aas_total_scale aas_depend_scale
MC1                1.00      0.79            0.01             0.00
ras_scale          0.79      1.00           -0.02            -0.01
aas_total_scale    0.01     -0.02            1.00             0.76
aas_depend_scale   0.00     -0.01            0.76             1.00
aas_anxiety_scale -0.05     -0.06            0.70             0.28
aas_close_scale    0.07      0.03            0.70             0.33
                  aas_anxiety_scale aas_close_scale
MC1                           -0.05            0.07
ras_scale                     -0.06            0.03
aas_total_scale                0.70            0.70
aas_depend_scale               0.28            0.33
aas_anxiety_scale              1.00            0.22
aas_close_scale                0.22            1.00
Sample Size 
[1] 167
Probability values (Entries above the diagonal are adjusted for multiple tests.) 
                   MC1 ras_scale aas_total_scale aas_depend_scale
MC1               0.00      0.00               1                1
ras_scale         0.00      0.00               1                1
aas_total_scale   0.90      0.79               0                0
aas_depend_scale  0.96      0.86               0                0
aas_anxiety_scale 0.52      0.45               0                0
aas_close_scale   0.35      0.68               0                0
                  aas_anxiety_scale aas_close_scale
MC1                               1            1.00
ras_scale                         1            1.00
aas_total_scale                   0            0.00
aas_depend_scale                  0            0.00
aas_anxiety_scale                 0            0.04
aas_close_scale                   0            0.00

 To see confidence intervals of the correlations, print with the short=FALSE option

Primary Analyses

This section reports the primary analyses used to test whether communication responsiveness condition, attachment and prior LDR experience group, and their interaction predicted relationship satisfaction scores.

Five 2 × 2 ANOVA models were estimated. All five models used relationship satisfaction as the dependent variable and communication responsiveness condition as the manipulated independent variable. In the first model, prior LDR experience group was the categorical moderator. In the second model, AAS Overall Insecurity was the continuous moderator. In the third model, AAS Dependence Insecurity was the continuous moderator. In the fourth model, AAS Attachment Anxiety was the continuous moderator. Lastly, in the fifth model, AAS Closeness Discomfort was the continuous moderator.

Model 1: Communication Responsiveness × Prior Relationship Experience

A 2 × 2 ANOVA tested whether relationship satisfaction scores differed by communication responsiveness condition, prior LDR experience group, and the communication responsiveness condition × prior LDR experience group interaction.

Show code 
a1_ras_relationship <- afex::aov_ez(
  id = "id",
  dv = "ras_scale",
  between = c("condition", "relationship_exp"),
  data = study_data
)

a1_ras_relationship
Anova Table (Type 3 tests)

Response: ras_scale
                      Effect     df  MSE          F  ges p.value
1                  condition 1, 162 0.61 102.10 *** .387   <.001
2           relationship_exp 1, 162 0.61       0.47 .003    .495
3 condition:relationship_exp 1, 162 0.61       1.62 .010    .205
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '+' 0.1 ' ' 1

The ANOVA table reports the main effect of communication responsiveness condition, the main effect of prior LDR experience group, and the communication responsiveness condition × prior LDR experience group interaction for relationship satisfaction scores.

Estimated Means for Communication Responsiveness × Prior Relationship Experience

Show code 
emmeans::emmeans(a1_ras_relationship, ~ condition)
 condition emmean     SE  df lower.CL upper.CL
 Low         2.64 0.0865 162     2.47     2.81
 High        3.90 0.0906 162     3.72     4.08

Results are averaged over the levels of: relationship_exp 
Confidence level used: 0.95
Show code 
emmeans::emmeans(a1_ras_relationship, ~ relationship_exp)
 relationship_exp emmean     SE  df lower.CL upper.CL
 No                 3.23 0.0950 162     3.04     3.42
 Yes                3.31 0.0816 162     3.15     3.47

Results are averaged over the levels of: condition 
Confidence level used: 0.95
Show code 
emmeans::emmeans(a1_ras_relationship, ~ condition * relationship_exp)
 condition relationship_exp emmean    SE  df lower.CL upper.CL
 Low       No                 2.67 0.119 162     2.44     2.91
 High      No                 3.78 0.148 162     3.49     4.07
 Low       Yes                2.60 0.125 162     2.35     2.85
 High      Yes                4.03 0.105 162     3.82     4.23

Confidence level used: 0.95

The estimated means show the relationship satisfaction score pattern across communication responsiveness condition, prior LDR experience group, and their combined cells.

Model 2: Communication Responsiveness × Overall Attachment Insecurity

A 2 × 2 ANOVA tested whether relationship satisfaction scores differed by communication responsiveness condition, AAS Overall Insecurity group, and the communication responsiveness condition × AAS Overall Insecurity group interaction.

Show code 
a2_ras_aas_total <- afex::aov_ez(
  id = "id",
  dv = "ras_scale",
  between = c("condition", "aas_total_group"),
  data = study_data
)

a2_ras_aas_total
Anova Table (Type 3 tests)

Response: ras_scale
                     Effect     df  MSE          F  ges p.value
1                 condition 1, 163 0.59 121.52 *** .427   <.001
2           aas_total_group 1, 163 0.59     6.44 * .038    .012
3 condition:aas_total_group 1, 163 0.59       0.23 .001    .634
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '+' 0.1 ' ' 1

The ANOVA table reports the main effect of communication responsiveness condition, the main effect of AAS Overall Insecurity group, and the communication responsiveness condition × AAS Overall Insecurity group interaction for relationship satisfaction scores.

Estimated Means for Communication Responsiveness × Overall Attachment Insecurity

Show code 
emmeans::emmeans(a2_ras_aas_total, ~ condition)
 condition emmean     SE  df lower.CL upper.CL
 Low         2.62 0.0848 163     2.46     2.79
 High        3.94 0.0841 163     3.77     4.11

Results are averaged over the levels of: aas_total_group 
Confidence level used: 0.95
Show code 
emmeans::emmeans(a2_ras_aas_total, ~ aas_total_group)
 aas_total_group              emmean     SE  df lower.CL upper.CL
 Lower attachment insecurity    3.43 0.0821 163     3.27     3.59
 Higher attachment insecurity   3.13 0.0867 163     2.96     3.30

Results are averaged over the levels of: condition 
Confidence level used: 0.95
Show code 
emmeans::emmeans(a2_ras_aas_total, ~ condition * aas_total_group)
 condition aas_total_group              emmean    SE  df lower.CL upper.CL
 Low       Lower attachment insecurity    2.75 0.115 163     2.52     2.97
 High      Lower attachment insecurity    4.12 0.117 163     3.89     4.35
 Low       Higher attachment insecurity   2.50 0.125 163     2.25     2.75
 High      Higher attachment insecurity   3.76 0.120 163     3.52     4.00

Confidence level used: 0.95

The estimated means show the relationship satisfaction score pattern across communication responsiveness condition, AAS Overall Insecurity group, and their combined cells.

Model 3: Communication Responsiveness × Dependence Insecurity

A 2 × 2 ANOVA tested whether relationship satisfaction scores differed by communication responsiveness condition, AAS Dependence Insecurity group, and the communication responsiveness condition × AAS Dependence Insecurity group interaction.

Show code 
a3_ras_aas_depend <- afex::aov_ez(
  id = "id",
  dv = "ras_scale",
  between = c("condition", "aas_depend_group"),
  data = study_data
)

a3_ras_aas_depend
Anova Table (Type 3 tests)

Response: ras_scale
                      Effect     df  MSE          F  ges p.value
1                  condition 1, 163 0.58 123.21 *** .430   <.001
2           aas_depend_group 1, 163 0.58    9.79 ** .057    .002
3 condition:aas_depend_group 1, 163 0.58       0.60 .004    .438
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '+' 0.1 ' ' 1

The ANOVA table reports the main effect of communication responsiveness condition, the main effect of AAS Dependence Insecurity group, and the communication responsiveness condition × AAS Dependence Insecurity group interaction for relationship satisfaction scores.

Estimated Means for Communication Responsiveness × Dependence Insecurity

Show code 
emmeans::emmeans(a3_ras_aas_depend, ~ condition)
 condition emmean     SE  df lower.CL upper.CL
 Low         2.59 0.0850 163     2.42     2.76
 High        3.92 0.0843 163     3.75     4.09

Results are averaged over the levels of: aas_depend_group 
Confidence level used: 0.95
Show code 
emmeans::emmeans(a3_ras_aas_depend, ~ aas_depend_group)
 aas_depend_group             emmean     SE  df lower.CL upper.CL
 Lower dependence insecurity    3.44 0.0770 163     3.29     3.60
 Higher dependence insecurity   3.07 0.0917 163     2.89     3.25

Results are averaged over the levels of: condition 
Confidence level used: 0.95
Show code 
emmeans::emmeans(a3_ras_aas_depend, ~ condition * aas_depend_group)
 condition aas_depend_group             emmean    SE  df lower.CL upper.CL
 Low       Lower dependence insecurity    2.83 0.109 163     2.61     3.04
 High      Lower dependence insecurity    4.06 0.109 163     3.85     4.28
 Low       Higher dependence insecurity   2.36 0.131 163     2.10     2.62
 High      Higher dependence insecurity   3.78 0.129 163     3.53     4.03

Confidence level used: 0.95

The estimated means show the relationship satisfaction score pattern across communication responsiveness condition, AAS Dependence Insecurity group, and their combined cells.

Model 4: Communication Responsiveness × Attachment Anxiety

A 2 × 2 ANOVA tested whether relationship satisfaction scores differed by communication responsiveness condition, AAS Attachment Anxiety group, and the communication responsiveness condition × AAS Attachment Anxiety group interaction.

Show code 
a4_ras_aas_anxiety <- afex::aov_ez(
  id = "id",
  dv = "ras_scale",
  between = c("condition", "aas_anxiety_group"),
  data = study_data
)

a4_ras_aas_anxiety
Anova Table (Type 3 tests)

Response: ras_scale
                       Effect     df  MSE          F   ges p.value
1                   condition 1, 163 0.61 118.04 ***  .420   <.001
2           aas_anxiety_group 1, 163 0.61       1.49  .009    .225
3 condition:aas_anxiety_group 1, 163 0.61       0.03 <.001    .857
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '+' 0.1 ' ' 1

The ANOVA table reports the main effect of communication responsiveness condition, the main effect of AAS Attachment Anxiety group, and the communication responsiveness condition × AAS Attachment Anxiety group interaction for relationship satisfaction scores.

Estimated Means for Communication Responsiveness × Attachment Anxiety

Show code

 Low         2.63 0.0862 163     2.46     2.80
 High        3.94 0.0853 163     3.78     4.11

Results are averaged over the levels of: aas_anxiety_group 
Confidence level used: 0.95

 Lower attachment anxiety    3.36 0.0839 163     3.19     3.52
 Higher attachment anxiety   3.21 0.0876 163     3.04     3.38

Results are averaged over the levels of: condition 
Confidence level used: 0.95
Show code

 condition aas_anxiety_group         emmean    SE  df lower.CL upper.CL
 Low       Lower attachment anxiety    2.71 0.117 163     2.48     2.94
 High      Lower attachment anxiety    4.01 0.121 163     3.77     4.25
 Low       Higher attachment anxiety   2.54 0.127 163     2.29     2.79

Model 5: Communication Responsiveness × Closeness Discomfort

A 2 × 2 ANOVA tested whether relationship satisfaction scores differed by communication responsiveness condition, AAS Closeness Discomfort group, and the communication responsiveness condition × AAS Closeness Discomfort group interaction.

Show code 
a5_ras_aas_close <- afex::aov_ez(
  id = "id",
  dv = "ras_scale",
  between = c("condition", "aas_close_group"),
  data = study_data
)

a5_ras_aas_close
Anova Table (Type 3 tests)

Response: ras_scale
                     Effect     df  MSE          F  ges p.value
1                 condition 1, 163 0.58 115.02 *** .414   <.001
2           aas_close_group 1, 163 0.58       0.92 .006    .339
3 condition:aas_close_group 1, 163 0.58    9.04 ** .053    .003
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '+' 0.1 ' ' 1

The ANOVA table reports the main effect of communication responsiveness condition, the main effect of AAS Closeness Discomfort group, and the communication responsiveness condition × AAS Closeness Discomfort group interaction for relationship satisfaction scores.

Estimated Means for Communication Responsiveness × Closeness Discomfort

Show code

 condition emmean     SE  df lower.CL upper.CL
 Low         2.66 0.0851 163     2.49     2.82
 High        3.93 0.0833 163     3.77     4.10

Results are averaged over the levels of: aas_close_group 
Confidence level used: 0.95
Show code

 aas_close_group             emmean     SE  df lower.CL upper.CL
 Lower closeness discomfort    3.35 0.0792 163     3.19     3.51
 Higher closeness discomfort   3.24 0.0889 163     3.06     3.41

Results are averaged over the levels of: condition 
Confidence level used: 0.95
Show code 
emmeans::emmeans(a5_ras_aas_close, ~ condition * aas_close_group)
 condition aas_close_group             emmean    SE  df lower.CL upper.CL
 Low       Lower closeness discomfort    2.53 0.109 163     2.32     2.75
 High      Lower closeness discomfort    4.17 0.115 163     3.94     4.40
 Low       Higher closeness discomfort   2.78 0.131 163     2.52     3.04
 High      Higher closeness discomfort   3.70 0.121 163     3.46     3.93

Confidence level used: 0.95

The estimated means show the relationship satisfaction score pattern across communication responsiveness condition, AAS Closeness Discomfort group, and their combined cells.

Figures

This section creates the figures used to visualize the primary 2 × 2 ANOVA results. The figures are based on the estimated cell means from the ANOVA models.

Relationship Satisfaction with Prior Relationship Experience Interaction Plot

Show code 
p_ras_relationship_bar <- ggplot(
  as.data.frame(emmeans(a1_ras_relationship, ~ condition * relationship_exp)),
  aes(
    x = condition,
    y = emmean,
    fill = relationship_exp
  )
) +
  geom_col(
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.65),
    width = 0.55,
    color = "white"
  ) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(
      ymin = lower.CL,
      ymax = upper.CL
    ),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.65),
    width = 0.12,
    linewidth = 0.7
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Communication Responsiveness Condition",
    y = "Mean Relationship Satisfaction Score",
    fill = "Relationship Experience"
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(
      "No" = "#C05780",   # YOU CAN CHANGE THESE TWO HEX VALUES TO MATCH YOUR POSTER!
      "Yes" = "#6C88C4"
    )
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    breaks = seq(1, 5, 1)
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(
    ylim = c(1, 5)
  ) +
  theme_classic(
    base_size = 14
  )

p_ras_relationship_bar

The figure shows estimated relationship satisfaction scores by communication responsiveness condition and prior LDR experience group. Error bars represent 95% confidence intervals.

Relationship Satisfaction with Overall Attachment Insecurity Interaction Plot

Show code 
p_ras_aas_total_bar <- ggplot(
  as.data.frame(emmeans(a2_ras_aas_total, ~ condition * aas_total_group)),
  aes(
    x = condition,
    y = emmean,
    fill = aas_total_group
  )
) +
  geom_col(
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.65),
    width = 0.55,
    color = "white"
  ) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(
      ymin = lower.CL,
      ymax = upper.CL
    ),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.65),
    width = 0.12,
    linewidth = 0.7
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Communication Responsiveness Condition",
    y = "Mean Relationship Satisfaction Score",
    fill = "Overall Attachment Insecurity"
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(
      "Lower attachment insecurity" = "#E76F51",   # YOU CAN CHANGE THESE TWO HEX VALUES TO MATCH YOUR POSTER!
      "Higher attachment insecurity" = "#2A9D8F"
    )
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    breaks = seq(1, 5, 1)
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(
    ylim = c(1, 5)
  ) +
  theme_classic(
    base_size = 14
  )

p_ras_aas_total_bar

The figure shows estimated relationship satisfaction scores by communication responsiveness condition and AAS Overall Insecurity group. Error bars represent 95% confidence intervals.

Relationship Satisfaction with Dependence Insecurity Interaction Plot

    breaks = seq(1, 5, 1)

  ) +

  )

p_ras_aas_anxiety_bar

The figure shows estimated relationship satisfaction scores by communication responsiveness condition and AAS Attachment Anxiety group. Error bars represent 95% confidence intervals.

Relationship Satisfaction with Closeness Discomfort Interaction Plot

  as.data.frame(emmeans(a5_ras_aas_close, ~ condition * aas_close_group)),
  aes(

p_ras_aas_close_bar

The figure shows estimated relationship satisfaction scores by communication responsiveness condition and AAS Closeness Discomfort group. Error bars represent 95% confidence intervals.

Export Figures

Show code 
if (!dir.exists("figures")) {
  dir.create("figures")
}

ggsave(
  filename = "figures/relationship_satisfaction_relationship_experience_interaction_plot.svg",
  plot = p_ras_relationship_bar,
  width = 7,
  height = 5
)

ggsave(
  filename = "figures/relationship_satisfaction_overall_attachment_insecurity_interaction_plot.svg",
  plot = p_ras_aas_total_bar,
  width = 7,
  height = 5
)

ggsave(
  filename = "figures/relationship_satisfaction_dependence_insecurity_interaction_plot.svg",
  plot = p_ras_aas_depend_bar,
  width = 7,
  height = 5
)

ggsave(
  filename = "figures/relationship_satisfaction_attachment_anxiety_interaction_plot.svg",
  plot = p_ras_aas_anxiety_bar,
  width = 7,
  height = 5
)

ggsave(
  filename = "figures/relationship_satisfaction_closeness_discomfort_interaction_plot.svg",
  plot = p_ras_aas_close_bar,
  width = 7,
  height = 5
)

The figures were exported to the figures/ folder so they can be used in manuscript, poster, or repository materials.

Results Summary

The primary analyses tested whether communication responsiveness condition, attachment and prior LDR experience groups, and their interaction predicted relationship satisfaction scores.

In the first ANOVA model, communication responsiveness was the only statistically significant predictor of relationship satisfaction. Participants in the high responsiveness condition reported higher relationship satisfaction than participants in the low responsiveness condition. Prior LDR experience and the interaction effect were insignificant. This suggests that prior LDR experience did not predict relationship satisfaction, as well as the difference between high and low responsiveness conditions did not meaningfully vary across prior LDR experience groups.

Furthermore, in the second ANOVA, communication responsiveness was significant along with AAS Overall Insecurity. Participants with lower overall insecurity reported higher relationship satisfaction than those in with higher overall insecurity. The interaction between communication responsiveness and AAS Overall Insecurity was insignificant. This indicates that the difference between high and low responsiveness conditions did not meaningfully vary across Overall Attachment Insecurity groups.

In the third ANOVA, communication responsiveness was significant along with AAS Dependence Insecurity. Participants with lower dependence insecurity reported higher relationship satisfaction than those in with higher dependence insecurity. The interaction between communication responsiveness and AAS Dependence Insecurity was insignificant. This means that the difference between high and low responsiveness conditions did not meaningfully vary across Dependence Insecurity groups.

In the fourth ANOVA, only communication responsiveness was significant. AAS Attachment Anxiety and the interaction between communication responsiveness and AAS Attachment Anxiety were insignificant. This suggests that Attachment Anxiety did not predict relationship satisfaction, as well as the difference between high and low responsiveness conditions did not meaningfully vary across Attachment Anxiety groups.

Finally, in the fifth ANOVA, communication responsiveness was significant along with the interaction between communication responsiveness and AAS Closeness Discomfort. This indicates that the difference between high and low responsiveness conditions meaningfully varied across Closeness Discomfort groups. However, Closeness Discomfort was insignificant, suggesting that Closeness Discomfort is not a predictor of relationship satisfaction.

The estimated means and interaction plots above provide the full model output and visualize the pattern of results for each outcome.

Reproducibility Information

The purpose of this section is to record everything in this Rstudio report and the packages that were used to render. Adding this information and instructions for the R code, the analyses will be able to be reproduced by other readers.

Show code 
sessionInfo()

Running under: macOS 15.6.1

Matrix products: default

[1] en_US.UTF-8/en_US.UTF-8/en_US.UTF-8/C/en_US.UTF-8/en_US.UTF-8

Source Code
---

## About This Report

The following demonstrates the workflow for the study, "Experiences in Long-Distance Relationships." This report documents the data preparation, variable construction, descriptive statistics, primary analyses, and figures for this study.

The report is intended to accompany a manuscript-style research paper. It is not the full paper itself. Instead, it shows how the reported analyses were produced from the dataset using a transparent and reproducible Quarto workflow.

## Study Overview

This study examines how perceived communication responsiveness in long-distance relationships affect relationship satisfaction, and if this association differs based on attachment style and prior experience in long-distance relationships.

The design included one independent variable and two moderators:

- Communication responsiveness condition: high communication responsiveness versus low communication responsiveness
- Attachment style: low attachment insecurity versus high attachment insecurity
- Prior LDR experience: experience versus no experience

The dependent variable was relationship satisfaction

The first primary analyses tested whether perceived communication responsiveness, attachment insecurity, and their interaction predicted relationship satisfaction. The attachment insecurity moderator included four subscales: overall insecurity, dependence insecurity, attachment anxiety, and closeness discomfort.

The second analyses evaluated whether perceived communication responsiveness, prior LDR experience, and their interaction predicted relationship satisfaction.

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo = TRUE,
  message = FALSE,
  warning = FALSE,
  fig.align = "center"
)

options(stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Setup and Software Environment

The purpose of this section is to load packages used for data preparation, scale construction, statistical modeling, estimated marginal means, and figures.

**Load Packages**

```{r}
#| label: load_packages
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

library(tidyverse)  # data manipulation + plotting
library(psych)      # scale construction + reliability
library(afex)       # ANOVA models + effect sizes
library(emmeans)    # estimated marginal means + confidence intervals
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Data Source and Analysis Dataset

The dataset was imported from the project `data` folder. The imported dataset was saved as `study_raw` so that the original file remained unchanged throughout the report.

A smaller analysis dataset, saved as `study_data`, was then created for the current analyses. This dataset retained the study condition variables, demographic variables, Relationship Assessment Scale items, Adult Attachment Scale items, and prior LDR experience items needed for variable preparation and analysis.

### Import Dataset

```{r}
#| label: import_data
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

study_raw <- read_csv("data/long_distance_data.csv")
```

### Check Imported Dataset

```{r}
#| label: check_imported_data
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

dim(study_raw)

names(study_raw)

head(study_raw)
```

Checking the imported dataset confirms that the data loaded successfully, shows the number of rows and columns, and displays the correct variable names available in the original dataset.

### Create Analysis Dataset

```{r}
#| label: create_analysis_dataset
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

study_vars <- c(
  "condition", "age", "gender", 
  
  
  "MC1",
  
  "RAS1", "RAS2", "RAS3", "RAS4", "RAS5", "RAS6", "RAS7",
  
  "AAS1", "AAS2", "AAS3", "AAS4", "AAS5", "AAS6",
  "AAS7", "AAS8", "AAS9", "AAS10", "AAS11", "AAS12",
  "AAS13", "AAS14", "AAS15", "AAS16", "AAS17", "AAS18",
  
  "relationship_exp",
  "relationship_length"
)

study_data <- study_raw %>%
  select(all_of(study_vars)) %>%
  mutate(
    across(everything(), as.numeric)
  )
```

### Check Analysis Dataset

```{r}
#| label: check_analysis_dataset
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

length(study_vars)

dim(study_data)

names(study_data)
```

The analysis dataset contains the variables required for the report. The checks confirm the number of selected variables, the dimensions of the analysis dataset, and the final variable names carried forward into the preparation and analysis sections.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Variable Preparation

This section prepares the variables used in the primary analyses. The original imported dataset was left unchanged; all prepared variables were created in the working analysis dataset, `study_data`.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Prepare Participant ID and Experimental Condition

```{r}
#| label: prepare_id_and_condition
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

study_data <- study_data %>%
  mutate(
    id = row_number(),

    condition = factor(
      condition,
      levels = c(0, 1),
      labels = c("Low", "High")
    ),

    relationship_exp = factor(
      relationship_exp,
      levels = c(0, 1),
      labels = c("No", "Yes")
    ),

    gender = factor(
      gender,
      levels = c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5),
      labels = c(
        "Female",
        "Male",
        "Transgender",
        "Prefer not to say",
        "Other"
      )
    )
  )
```

A participant ID variable was created so that each row in the analysis dataset had a unique identifier. The experiemental condition variable was recoded as a labeled factor with two levels: Low and High. The moderator, prior LDR experience was also recoded as a labels factor with two levles: No and Yes.

### Check Experimental Condition Coding

```{r}
#| label: check_factor_categories
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

study_data %>%
  count(condition) %>%
  knitr::kable()

study_data %>%
  count(relationship_exp) %>%
  knitr::kable()

study_data %>%
  count(gender) %>%
  knitr::kable()
```

The condition check confirms the number of participants assigned to each communication responsivenss condition. The moderator check also confirms the number of participants that have and do not have LDR experience. Lastly, gender is confirmed to analyze demographics.

### Check Prepared Analysis Dataset

```{r}
#| label: check_prepared_analysis_dataset
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

dim(study_data)

names(study_data)
```

The prepared analysis dataset contains the variables needed for scale construction, descriptive statistics, and primary analyses.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Reverse-Code Scale Items

Items requiring reverse coding were transformed before scale scores were computed. Because the items in both scales used a 1–5 response scale, reverse-coded items were created by subtracting each original item response from 6.

```{r}
#| label: reverse_code_items
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

study_data <- study_data %>%
  mutate(
    # Reverse-code RAS items
    # Original scale: 1 to 5
    # Higher recoded scores = higher relationship satisfaction
    RAS4r = 6 - RAS4,
    RAS7r = 6 - RAS7,

    # Reverse-code AAS items
    # Original scale: 1 to 5
    # Higher recoded scores = higher attachment dimension score
    AAS3r  = 6 - AAS3,
    AAS4r  = 6 - AAS4,
    AAS7r  = 6 - AAS7,
    AAS13r = 6 - AAS13,
    AAS14r = 6 - AAS14,
    AAS17r = 6 - AAS17
  )
```

### Check Reverse Coding

```{r}
#| label: check_reverse_coded_items
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

study_data %>%
  select(RAS4, RAS4r, RAS7, RAS7r, AAS3, AAS3r, AAS7, AAS7r) %>%
  head(10) %>%
  knitr::kable()
```

### Create Scale Scores

Scale item lists were defined and used to compute Relationship Assessment and Adult Attachment scale scores.

```{r}
#| label: create_scale_scores
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

ras_items <- c("RAS1", "RAS2", "RAS3", "RAS4r", "RAS5", "RAS6", "RAS7r")

aas_total_items <- c("AAS1", "AAS2", "AAS3r", "AAS4r", "AAS5", 
                     "AAS6", "AAS7r", "AAS8", "AAS9", "AAS10", 
                     "AAS11", "AAS12", "AAS13r", "AAS14r", "AAS15", 
                     "AAS16", "AAS17r", "AAS18")

aas_depend_items <- c("AAS1", "AAS2", "AAS3r", "AAS4r", "AAS5", "AAS6")

aas_anxiety_items <- c("AAS7r", "AAS8", "AAS9", "AAS10", "AAS11", "AAS12")

aas_close_items <- c("AAS13r", "AAS14r", "AAS15", "AAS16", "AAS17r", "AAS18")

study_data <- study_data %>%
  mutate(
    ras_scale = rowMeans(select(., all_of(ras_items)), na.rm = TRUE),
    aas_total_scale = rowMeans(select(., all_of(aas_total_items)), na.rm = TRUE),
    aas_depend_scale = rowMeans(select(., all_of(aas_depend_items)), na.rm = TRUE),
    aas_anxiety_scale = rowMeans(select(., all_of(aas_anxiety_items)), na.rm = TRUE),
    aas_close_scale = rowMeans(select(., all_of(aas_close_items)), na.rm = TRUE)
  )
```

### Check Scale Scores

```{r}
#| label: check_scale_scores
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

study_data %>%
  select(
    ras_scale,
    aas_total_scale,
    aas_depend_scale,
    aas_anxiety_scale,
    aas_close_scale
  ) %>%
  summary()

study_data %>%
  summarise(
    ras_scale_missing = mean(is.na(ras_scale)),
    aas_total_scale_missing = mean(is.na(aas_total_scale)),
    aas_depend_scale_missing = mean(is.na(aas_depend_scale)),
    aas_anxiety_scale_missing = mean(is.na(aas_anxiety_scale)),
    aas_close_scale_missing = mean(is.na(aas_close_scale))
  ) %>%
  knitr::kable()
```

The scale-score checks confirm that the computed scale scores fall within the expected response range and show whether any scale scores are missing.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Create Attachment Groups

A Lower/Higher attachment insecurity grouping variable was created for the planned 2 × 2 ANOVA models. Participants with attachment scores at or below the sample median were assigned to the Lower Attachment Insecurity group, and participants above the sample median were assigned to the Higher Attachment Insecurity group. The same was also done for Lower/High dependence insecurity, attachment anxiety, and closeness discomfort.

```{r}
#| label: create_attachment_groups
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

# Create median values for the attachment scale scores
aas_total_median <- median(study_data$aas_total_scale, na.rm = TRUE)
aas_depend_median <- median(study_data$aas_depend_scale, na.rm = TRUE)
aas_anxiety_median <- median(study_data$aas_anxiety_scale, na.rm = TRUE)
aas_close_median <- median(study_data$aas_close_scale, na.rm = TRUE)

# Create median-split attachment groups
study_data <- study_data %>%
  mutate(
    aas_total_group = if_else(
      aas_total_scale <= aas_total_median,
      "Lower attachment insecurity",
      "Higher attachment insecurity"
    ),
    aas_total_group = factor(
      aas_total_group,
      levels = c("Lower attachment insecurity", "Higher attachment insecurity")
    ),

    aas_depend_group = if_else(
      aas_depend_scale <= aas_depend_median,
      "Lower dependence insecurity",
      "Higher dependence insecurity"
    ),
    aas_depend_group = factor(
      aas_depend_group,
      levels = c("Lower dependence insecurity", "Higher dependence insecurity")
    ),

    aas_anxiety_group = if_else(
      aas_anxiety_scale <= aas_anxiety_median,
      "Lower attachment anxiety",
      "Higher attachment anxiety"
    ),
    aas_anxiety_group = factor(
      aas_anxiety_group,
      levels = c("Lower attachment anxiety", "Higher attachment anxiety")
    ),

    aas_close_group = if_else(
      aas_close_scale <= aas_close_median,
      "Lower closeness discomfort",
      "Higher closeness discomfort"
    ),
    aas_close_group = factor(
      aas_close_group,
      levels = c("Lower closeness discomfort", "Higher closeness discomfort")
    )
  )

# Check final attachment group sizes
study_data %>%
  count(aas_total_group) %>%
  knitr::kable()

study_data %>%
  count(aas_depend_group) %>%
  knitr::kable()

study_data %>%
  count(aas_anxiety_group) %>%
  knitr::kable()

study_data %>%
  count(aas_close_group) %>%
  knitr::kable()
```

The median split created the attachment grouping variable used in the primary ANOVA models. The output reports the median value used for the split and the number of participants assigned to each attachment group.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Check Scale Reliability

Internal consistency was estimated for the Relationship Assessment and Adult Attachment scales before the scale scores were used in the primary analyses

```{r}
#| label: check_scale_reliability
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

# Create item-only data frames for each multi-item rating scale
ras_item_df <- study_data %>% 
  select(all_of(ras_items))

aas_total_item_df <- study_data %>% 
  select(all_of(aas_total_items))

aas_depend_item_df <- study_data %>% 
  select(all_of(aas_depend_items))

aas_anxiety_item_df <- study_data %>% 
  select(all_of(aas_anxiety_items))

aas_close_item_df <- study_data %>% 
  select(all_of(aas_close_items))

# Compute internal consistency estimates
ras_alpha <- psych::alpha(ras_item_df)
ras_omega <- psych::omega(ras_item_df, plot = FALSE)

aas_total_alpha <- psych::alpha(aas_total_item_df)
aas_total_omega <- psych::omega(aas_total_item_df, plot = FALSE)

aas_depend_alpha <- psych::alpha(aas_depend_item_df)
aas_depend_omega <- psych::omega(aas_depend_item_df, plot = FALSE)

aas_anxiety_alpha <- psych::alpha(aas_anxiety_item_df)
aas_anxiety_omega <- psych::omega(aas_anxiety_item_df, plot = FALSE)

aas_close_alpha <- psych::alpha(aas_close_item_df)
aas_close_omega <- psych::omega(aas_close_item_df, plot = FALSE)

# Print only alpha and omega coefficients
tibble(
  scale = c(
    "Relationship Assessment Scale",
    "AAS Overall Insecurity",
    "AAS Dependence Insecurity",
    "AAS Attachment Anxiety",
    "AAS Closeness Discomfort"
  ),
  alpha = c(
    ras_alpha$total$raw_alpha,
    aas_total_alpha$total$raw_alpha,
    aas_depend_alpha$total$raw_alpha,
    aas_anxiety_alpha$total$raw_alpha,
    aas_close_alpha$total$raw_alpha
  ),
  omega = c(
    ras_omega$omega.tot,
    aas_total_omega$omega.tot,
    aas_depend_omega$omega.tot,
    aas_anxiety_omega$omega.tot,
    aas_close_omega$omega.tot
  )
) %>%
  knitr::kable(digits = 2)
```

The reliability table reports Cronbach’s alpha and McDonald’s omega for each scale used in the analysis.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Descriptive Statistics

This section summarizes the scale scores and sample composition before the primary hypothesis tests.

### Scale Score Descriptives

```{r}
#| label: scale_score_descriptives
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

study_data %>%
  select(
    ras_scale,
    aas_total_scale,
    aas_depend_scale,
    aas_anxiety_scale,
    aas_close_scale
  ) %>%
  psych::describe() %>%
  knitr::kable(digits = 2)
```

The table reports descriptive statistics for the Relationship Assessment and Adult Attachment scale scores used in the primary analyses.

### Sample Composition by Age and Gender

```{r}
#| label: sample_composition_demographic_variables
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

# Recode age categories so the Qualtrics numeric codes show as actual age labels
study_data <- study_data %>%
  mutate(
    age = factor(
      age,
      levels = c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8),
      labels = c("18", "19", "20", "21", "22", "23", "24", "Other")
    )
  )

# Summarize age categories
study_data %>%
  count(age) %>%
  mutate(percent = round(100 * n / sum(n), 1)) %>%
  knitr::kable(
    col.names = c("Age", "n", "Percent"),
    caption = "Sample Composition by Age"
  )

# Summarize gender
study_data %>%
  count(gender) %>%
  mutate(percent = round(100 * n / sum(n), 1)) %>%
  knitr::kable(
    col.names = c("Gender", "n", "Percent"),
    caption = "Sample Composition by Gender"
  )

# Summarize prior relationship experience
study_data %>%
  count(relationship_exp) %>%
  mutate(percent = round(100 * n / sum(n), 1)) %>%
  knitr::kable(
    col.names = c("Prior Relationship Experience", "n", "Percent"),
    caption = "Sample Composition by Prior Relationship Experience"
  )

# Summarize experimental condition
study_data %>%
  count(condition) %>%
  mutate(percent = round(100 * n / sum(n), 1)) %>%
  knitr::kable(
    col.names = c("Condition", "n", "Percent"),
    caption = "Sample Composition by Experimental Condition"
  )
```

The tables report the number and percentage of participants in each age, gender, prior LDR experience, and experimental condition category.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Correlations Among the DVs

This section tests the correlation among the continuous variables in the study, which are the manipulation check question, Relationship Assessment Scale, AAS Overall Insecurity, AAS Dependence Insecurity, AAS Attachment Anxiety, and AAS Closeness Discomfort.

```{r}
#| label: correlation_test
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

# Correlations among the main continuous study variables
psych::corr.test(
  study_data %>% 
    select(
      MC1,
      ras_scale,
      aas_total_scale,
      aas_depend_scale,
      aas_anxiety_scale,
      aas_close_scale
    )
)
```

## Primary Analyses

This section reports the primary analyses used to test whether communication responsiveness condition, attachment and prior LDR experience group, and their interaction predicted relationship satisfaction scores.

Five 2 × 2 ANOVA models were estimated. All five models used relationship satisfaction as the dependent variable and communication responsiveness condition as the manipulated independent variable. In the first model, prior LDR experience group was the categorical moderator. In the second model, AAS Overall Insecurity was the continuous moderator. In the third model, AAS Dependence Insecurity was the continuous moderator. In the fourth model, AAS Attachment Anxiety was the continuous moderator. Lastly, in the fifth model, AAS Closeness Discomfort was the continuous moderator.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### **Model 1:** Communication Responsiveness × Prior Relationship Experience

A 2 × 2 ANOVA tested whether relationship satisfaction scores differed by communication responsiveness condition, prior LDR experience group, and the communication responsiveness condition × prior LDR experience group interaction.

```{r}
#| label: anova_ras_relationship
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

a1_ras_relationship <- afex::aov_ez(
  id = "id",
  dv = "ras_scale",
  between = c("condition", "relationship_exp"),
  data = study_data
)

a1_ras_relationship
```

The ANOVA table reports the main effect of communication responsiveness condition, the main effect of prior LDR experience group, and the communication responsiveness condition × prior LDR experience group interaction for relationship satisfaction scores.

### Estimated Means for Communication Responsiveness × Prior Relationship Experience

```{r}
#| label: estimated_means_ras_relationship
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

emmeans::emmeans(a1_ras_relationship, ~ condition)

emmeans::emmeans(a1_ras_relationship, ~ relationship_exp)

emmeans::emmeans(a1_ras_relationship, ~ condition * relationship_exp)
```

The estimated means show the relationship satisfaction score pattern across communication responsiveness condition, prior LDR experience group, and their combined cells.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Model 2: Communication Responsiveness × Overall Attachment Insecurity

A 2 × 2 ANOVA tested whether relationship satisfaction scores differed by communication responsiveness condition, AAS Overall Insecurity group, and the communication responsiveness condition × AAS Overall Insecurity group interaction.

```{r}
#| label: anova_ras_aas_total
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

a2_ras_aas_total <- afex::aov_ez(
  id = "id",
  dv = "ras_scale",
  between = c("condition", "aas_total_group"),
  data = study_data
)

a2_ras_aas_total
```

The ANOVA table reports the main effect of communication responsiveness condition, the main effect of AAS Overall Insecurity group, and the communication responsiveness condition × AAS Overall Insecurity group interaction for relationship satisfaction scores.

### Estimated Means for Communication Responsiveness × Overall Attachment Insecurity

```{r}
#| label: estimated_means_ras_aas_total
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

emmeans::emmeans(a2_ras_aas_total, ~ condition)

emmeans::emmeans(a2_ras_aas_total, ~ aas_total_group)

emmeans::emmeans(a2_ras_aas_total, ~ condition * aas_total_group)
```

The estimated means show the relationship satisfaction score pattern across communication responsiveness condition, AAS Overall Insecurity group, and their combined cells.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Model 3: Communication Responsiveness × Dependence Insecurity

A 2 × 2 ANOVA tested whether relationship satisfaction scores differed by communication responsiveness condition, AAS Dependence Insecurity group, and the communication responsiveness condition × AAS Dependence Insecurity group interaction.

```{r}
#| label: anova_ras_aas_depend
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

a3_ras_aas_depend <- afex::aov_ez(
  id = "id",
  dv = "ras_scale",
  between = c("condition", "aas_depend_group"),
  data = study_data
)

a3_ras_aas_depend
```

The ANOVA table reports the main effect of communication responsiveness condition, the main effect of AAS Dependence Insecurity group, and the communication responsiveness condition × AAS Dependence Insecurity group interaction for relationship satisfaction scores.

### Estimated Means for Communication Responsiveness × Dependence Insecurity

```{r}
#| label: estimated_means_ras_aas_depend
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

emmeans::emmeans(a3_ras_aas_depend, ~ condition)

emmeans::emmeans(a3_ras_aas_depend, ~ aas_depend_group)

emmeans::emmeans(a3_ras_aas_depend, ~ condition * aas_depend_group)
```

The estimated means show the relationship satisfaction score pattern across communication responsiveness condition, AAS Dependence Insecurity group, and their combined cells.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Model 4: Communication Responsiveness × Attachment Anxiety

A 2 × 2 ANOVA tested whether relationship satisfaction scores differed by communication responsiveness condition, AAS Attachment Anxiety group, and the communication responsiveness condition × AAS Attachment Anxiety group interaction.

```{r}
#| label: anova_ras_aas_anxiety
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

a4_ras_aas_anxiety <- afex::aov_ez(
  id = "id",
  dv = "ras_scale",
  between = c("condition", "aas_anxiety_group"),
  data = study_data
)

a4_ras_aas_anxiety
```

The ANOVA table reports the main effect of communication responsiveness condition, the main effect of AAS Attachment Anxiety group, and the communication responsiveness condition × AAS Attachment Anxiety group interaction for relationship satisfaction scores.

### Estimated Means for Communication Responsiveness × Attachment Anxiety

```{r}
#| label: estimated_means_ras_aas_anxiety
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

emmeans::emmeans(a4_ras_aas_anxiety, ~ condition)

emmeans::emmeans(a4_ras_aas_anxiety, ~ aas_anxiety_group)

emmeans::emmeans(a4_ras_aas_anxiety, ~ condition * aas_anxiety_group)
```

The estimated means show the relationship satisfaction score pattern across communication responsiveness condition, AAS Attachment Anxiety group, and their combined cells.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Model 5: Communication Responsiveness × Closeness Discomfort

A 2 × 2 ANOVA tested whether relationship satisfaction scores differed by communication responsiveness condition, AAS Closeness Discomfort group, and the communication responsiveness condition × AAS Closeness Discomfort group interaction.

```{r}
#| label: anova_ras_aas_close
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

a5_ras_aas_close <- afex::aov_ez(
  id = "id",
  dv = "ras_scale",
  between = c("condition", "aas_close_group"),
  data = study_data
)

a5_ras_aas_close
```

The ANOVA table reports the main effect of communication responsiveness condition, the main effect of AAS Closeness Discomfort group, and the communication responsiveness condition × AAS Closeness Discomfort group interaction for relationship satisfaction scores.

### Estimated Means for Communication Responsiveness × Closeness Discomfort

```{r}
#| label: estimated_means_ras_aas_close
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

emmeans::emmeans(a5_ras_aas_close, ~ condition)

emmeans::emmeans(a5_ras_aas_close, ~ aas_close_group)

emmeans::emmeans(a5_ras_aas_close, ~ condition * aas_close_group)
```

The estimated means show the relationship satisfaction score pattern across communication responsiveness condition, AAS Closeness Discomfort group, and their combined cells.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Figures

This section creates the figures used to visualize the primary 2 × 2 ANOVA results. The figures are based on the estimated cell means from the ANOVA models.

### Relationship Satisfaction with Prior Relationship Experience Interaction Plot

```{r}
#| label: ras_relationship_interaction_plot
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

p_ras_relationship_bar <- ggplot(
  as.data.frame(emmeans(a1_ras_relationship, ~ condition * relationship_exp)),
  aes(
    x = condition,
    y = emmean,
    fill = relationship_exp
  )
) +
  geom_col(
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.65),
    width = 0.55,
    color = "white"
  ) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(
      ymin = lower.CL,
      ymax = upper.CL
    ),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.65),
    width = 0.12,
    linewidth = 0.7
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Communication Responsiveness Condition",
    y = "Mean Relationship Satisfaction Score",
    fill = "Relationship Experience"
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(
      "No" = "#C05780",   # YOU CAN CHANGE THESE TWO HEX VALUES TO MATCH YOUR POSTER!
      "Yes" = "#6C88C4"
    )
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    breaks = seq(1, 5, 1)
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(
    ylim = c(1, 5)
  ) +
  theme_classic(
    base_size = 14
  )

p_ras_relationship_bar
```

The figure shows estimated relationship satisfaction scores by communication responsiveness condition and prior LDR experience group. Error bars represent 95% confidence intervals.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Relationship Satisfaction with Overall Attachment Insecurity Interaction Plot

```{r}
#| label: ras_aas_total_interaction_plot
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

p_ras_aas_total_bar <- ggplot(
  as.data.frame(emmeans(a2_ras_aas_total, ~ condition * aas_total_group)),
  aes(
    x = condition,
    y = emmean,
    fill = aas_total_group
  )
) +
  geom_col(
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.65),
    width = 0.55,
    color = "white"
  ) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(
      ymin = lower.CL,
      ymax = upper.CL
    ),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.65),
    width = 0.12,
    linewidth = 0.7
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Communication Responsiveness Condition",
    y = "Mean Relationship Satisfaction Score",
    fill = "Overall Attachment Insecurity"
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(
      "Lower attachment insecurity" = "#E76F51",   # YOU CAN CHANGE THESE TWO HEX VALUES TO MATCH YOUR POSTER!
      "Higher attachment insecurity" = "#2A9D8F"
    )
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    breaks = seq(1, 5, 1)
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(
    ylim = c(1, 5)
  ) +
  theme_classic(
    base_size = 14
  )

p_ras_aas_total_bar
```

The figure shows estimated relationship satisfaction scores by communication responsiveness condition and AAS Overall Insecurity group. Error bars represent 95% confidence intervals.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Relationship Satisfaction with Dependence Insecurity Interaction Plot

```{r}
#| label: ras_aas_depend_interaction_plot
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

p_ras_aas_depend_bar <- ggplot(
  as.data.frame(emmeans(a3_ras_aas_depend, ~ condition * aas_depend_group)),
  aes(
    x = condition,
    y = emmean,
    fill = aas_depend_group
  )
) +
  geom_col(
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.65),
    width = 0.55,
    color = "white"
  ) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(
      ymin = lower.CL,
      ymax = upper.CL
    ),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.65),
    width = 0.12,
    linewidth = 0.7
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Communication Responsiveness Condition",
    y = "Mean Relationship Satisfaction Score",
    fill = "Dependence Insecurity"
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(
      "Lower dependence insecurity" = "#B03060",   # YOU CAN CHANGE THESE TWO HEX VALUES TO MATCH YOUR POSTER!
      "Higher dependence insecurity" = "#458B00"
    )
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    breaks = seq(1, 5, 1)
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(
    ylim = c(1, 5)
  ) +
  theme_classic(
    base_size = 14
  )

p_ras_aas_depend_bar
```

The figure shows estimated relationship satisfaction scores by communication responsiveness condition and AAS Dependence Insecurity group. Error bars represent 95% confidence intervals.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Relationship Satisfaction with Dependence Insecurity Interaction Plot

```{r}
#| label: ras_aas_anxiety_interaction_plot
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

p_ras_aas_anxiety_bar <- ggplot(
  as.data.frame(emmeans(a4_ras_aas_anxiety, ~ condition * aas_anxiety_group)),
  aes(
    x = condition,
    y = emmean,
    fill = aas_anxiety_group
  )
) +
  geom_col(
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.65),
    width = 0.55,
    color = "white"
  ) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(
      ymin = lower.CL,
      ymax = upper.CL
    ),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.65),
    width = 0.12,
    linewidth = 0.7
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Communication Responsiveness Condition",
    y = "Mean Relationship Satisfaction Score",
    fill = "Attachment Anxiety"
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(
      "Lower attachment anxiety" = "#FFB90F",   # YOU CAN CHANGE THESE TWO HEX VALUES TO MATCH YOUR POSTER!
      "Higher attachment anxiety" = "#27408B"
    )
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    breaks = seq(1, 5, 1)
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(
    ylim = c(1, 5)
  ) +
  theme_classic(
    base_size = 14
  )

p_ras_aas_anxiety_bar
```

The figure shows estimated relationship satisfaction scores by communication responsiveness condition and AAS Attachment Anxiety group. Error bars represent 95% confidence intervals.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Relationship Satisfaction with Closeness Discomfort Interaction Plot

```{r}
#| label: ras_aas_close_interaction_plot
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

p_ras_aas_close_bar <- ggplot(
  as.data.frame(emmeans(a5_ras_aas_close, ~ condition * aas_close_group)),
  aes(
    x = condition,
    y = emmean,
    fill = aas_close_group
  )
) +
  geom_col(
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.65),
    width = 0.55,
    color = "white"
  ) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(
      ymin = lower.CL,
      ymax = upper.CL
    ),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.65),
    width = 0.12,
    linewidth = 0.7
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Communication Responsiveness Condition",
    y = "Mean Relationship Satisfaction Score",
    fill = "Closeness Discomfort"
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(
      "Lower closeness discomfort" = "#EE7621",   # YOU CAN CHANGE THESE TWO HEX VALUES TO MATCH YOUR POSTER!
      "Higher closeness discomfort" = "#473C8B"
    )
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    breaks = seq(1, 5, 1)
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(
    ylim = c(1, 5)
  ) +
  theme_classic(
    base_size = 14
  )

p_ras_aas_close_bar
```

The figure shows estimated relationship satisfaction scores by communication responsiveness condition and AAS Closeness Discomfort group. Error bars represent 95% confidence intervals.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Export Figures

```{r}
#| label: save_figures
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

if (!dir.exists("figures")) {
  dir.create("figures")
}

ggsave(
  filename = "figures/relationship_satisfaction_relationship_experience_interaction_plot.svg",
  plot = p_ras_relationship_bar,
  width = 7,
  height = 5
)

ggsave(
  filename = "figures/relationship_satisfaction_overall_attachment_insecurity_interaction_plot.svg",
  plot = p_ras_aas_total_bar,
  width = 7,
  height = 5
)

ggsave(
  filename = "figures/relationship_satisfaction_dependence_insecurity_interaction_plot.svg",
  plot = p_ras_aas_depend_bar,
  width = 7,
  height = 5
)

ggsave(
  filename = "figures/relationship_satisfaction_attachment_anxiety_interaction_plot.svg",
  plot = p_ras_aas_anxiety_bar,
  width = 7,
  height = 5
)

ggsave(
  filename = "figures/relationship_satisfaction_closeness_discomfort_interaction_plot.svg",
  plot = p_ras_aas_close_bar,
  width = 7,
  height = 5
)
```

The figures were exported to the `figures/` folder so they can be used in manuscript, poster, or repository materials.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Results Summary

The primary analyses tested whether communication responsiveness condition, attachment and prior LDR experience groups, and their interaction predicted relationship satisfaction scores.

In the first ANOVA model, communication responsiveness was the only statistically significant predictor of relationship satisfaction. Participants in the high responsiveness condition reported higher relationship satisfaction than participants in the low responsiveness condition. Prior LDR experience and the interaction effect were insignificant. This suggests that prior LDR experience did not predict relationship satisfaction, as well as the difference between high and low responsiveness conditions did not meaningfully vary across prior LDR experience groups.

Furthermore, in the second ANOVA, communication responsiveness was significant along with AAS Overall Insecurity. Participants with lower overall insecurity reported higher relationship satisfaction than those in with higher overall insecurity. The interaction between communication responsiveness and AAS Overall Insecurity was insignificant. This indicates that the difference between high and low responsiveness conditions did not meaningfully vary across Overall Attachment Insecurity groups.

In the third ANOVA, communication responsiveness was significant along with AAS Dependence Insecurity. Participants with lower dependence insecurity reported higher relationship satisfaction than those in with higher dependence insecurity. The interaction between communication responsiveness and AAS Dependence Insecurity was insignificant. This means that the difference between high and low responsiveness conditions did not meaningfully vary across Dependence Insecurity groups.

In the fourth ANOVA, only communication responsiveness was significant. AAS Attachment Anxiety and the interaction between communication responsiveness and AAS Attachment Anxiety were insignificant. This suggests that Attachment Anxiety did not predict relationship satisfaction, as well as the difference between high and low responsiveness conditions did not meaningfully vary across Attachment Anxiety groups.

Finally, in the fifth ANOVA, communication responsiveness was significant along with the interaction between communication responsiveness and AAS Closeness Discomfort. This indicates that the difference between high and low responsiveness conditions meaningfully varied across Closeness Discomfort groups. However, Closeness Discomfort was insignificant, suggesting that Closeness Discomfort is not a predictor of relationship satisfaction.

The estimated means and interaction plots above provide the full model output and visualize the pattern of results for each outcome.

## Reproducibility Information

The purpose of this section is to record everything in this Rstudio report and the packages that were used to render. Adding this information and instructions for the R code, the analyses will be able to be reproduced by other readers.

```{r}
#| label: session_info
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

sessionInfo()
```
